section.title: A Cry from the Depths of Darkness
section.sectionRationale: The darkest psalm in the Psalter, this unrelieved lament of Heman the Ezrahite ends without resolution, in darkness alone, honestly voicing the anguish of a believer who feels forsaken by God and friends alike, a cry that anticipates and is dignified by Christ's own cry of dereliction on the cross.
section.thesis: Heman cries to the God of his salvation day and night from the edge of the grave, refusing to stop praying even though he receives no relief, modeling honest, unresolved lament that finds its deepest companion in Christ, who also cried out in darkness before the dawn of resurrection.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a persistent cry to the saving God, verses 1-2; a soul swallowed by trouble and God's wrath, verses 3-7; isolation from friends amid failing sight, verses 8-9; questions hurled from the edge of the grave, verses 10-12; and unrelieved affliction that ends in darkness, verses 13-18.

units.label: Day and Night I Cry Out
units.note: a persistent cry to the saving God

verses.text: O LORD, the God of my salvation, day and night I cry out before You.
May my prayer come before You; incline Your ear to my cry.
For my soul is full of troubles, and my life draws near to {{sheol|Sheol}}.
I am counted among those descending to the {{bor|Pit}}. I am like a man without strength.
I am forsaken among the dead, like the slain who lie in the grave, whom You remember no more, who are cut off from Your care.
You have laid me in the lowest Pit, in the darkest of the depths.
Your wrath weighs heavily upon me; all Your waves have submerged me. Selah
You have removed my friends from me; You have made me repulsive to them; I am confined and cannot escape.
My eyes grow dim with grief. I call to You daily, O LORD; I spread out my hands to You.
Do You work wonders for the dead? Do departed spirits rise up to praise You? Selah
Can Your {{chesed|loving devotion}} be proclaimed in the grave, Your faithfulness in {{abaddon|Abaddon}}?
Will Your wonders be known in the darkness, or Your righteousness in the land of oblivion?
But to You, O LORD, I cry for help; in the morning my prayer comes before You.
Why, O LORD, do You reject me? Why do You hide Your face from me?
From my youth I was afflicted and near death. I have borne Your terrors; I am in despair.
Your wrath has swept over me; Your terrors have destroyed me.
All day long they engulf me like water; they enclose me on every side.
You have removed my beloved and my friend; darkness is my closest companion.

sheol.gloss: The realm of the dead, the grave. Heman describes his suffering as bringing him to the very brink of this shadowy place of separation from the living.

bor.gloss: A pit or cistern, used as a picture of the grave. Heman feels himself already counted among those descending into this place of darkness and confinement.

abaddon.gloss: Destruction, a name for the place of the dead. Heman asks whether God's faithfulness can even be known in so desolate a place, later personified as a name for the realm of destruction in Revelation 9:11.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love. Heman questions, from the depth of his despair, whether this love of God can be proclaimed from the grave, a raw but faith-filled protest addressed still to the LORD.

basic.context: Heman the Ezrahite, a temple singer, cries out to God day and night from what feels like the very edge of the grave. Friends have vanished, God seems silent, and unlike almost every other psalm, this one ends with no resolution, no word of praise, only darkness.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes one believer's specific, prolonged anguish, and it prescribes an abiding truth: honest, unresolved lament addressed to God is itself an act of genuine faith, not a failure of it.
basic.originalLanguages: Heman describes his life drawing near to sheol, the realm of the dead, verse 3, and himself counted among those descending to the bor, the Pit, verse 4. He asks whether God's chesed, steadfast love, can be proclaimed even in abaddon, the place of destruction, verse 11.
basic.typology: Heman's cry of abandonment, his sense that God has hidden His face, and his experience of isolation from every friend anticipate Christ's own cry of dereliction from the cross and His descent into the darkness of death before the dawn of resurrection.
basic.christ: Jesus took up the raw honesty of this psalm's anguish when He cried, 'My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?' His disciples fled just as Heman's friends were removed from him, and He entered the true darkness of death so that believers who walk through their own unrelieved nights would never walk through them without a Savior who has already been there.
basic.god: God is addressed here as the God of salvation even in the midst of felt abandonment, showing that lament itself is an act of faith. The psalm does not resolve God's silence, but it never stops speaking to Him, honoring both His sovereignty over suffering and His nearness to those who cry out honestly.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 27:46 records Christ's own cry of abandonment, echoing this psalm's rawest anguish. Hebrews 5:7 describes Jesus offering prayers with loud cries and tears. Mark 14:50 shows the disciples fleeing, just as Heman's friends and companions were removed from him.
basic.oneStory: This unrelieved lament gives voice to the darkest nights believers face, and finds its deepest companion in Christ, who entered fully into human abandonment and the darkness of death, so that no cry from the depths is ever prayed alone.

context.literary: A maskil of Heman the Ezrahite, structured as an unbroken lament with no turn to praise, moving from persistent cry, verses 1-2, through deepening darkness, verses 3-9, rhetorical questions from the grave's edge, verses 10-12, to a final, unresolved cry, verses 13-18.
context.historical: Heman was one of the wise singers of Israel appointed to temple worship; his psalm was preserved and sung publicly despite its unrelieved darkness, showing that ancient Israel had room in its worship for honest, unresolved suffering.
context.canonical: Unique among the psalms for ending without any note of praise or resolution, this psalm gives permission within the canon itself for prayer that remains in lament, without demanding a tidy resolution before the prayer is considered faithful.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give voice, within Israel's worship, to the experience of prolonged suffering and felt abandonment by God, modeling honest address to God even when no relief or resolution comes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes Heman's own specific and prolonged affliction; it prescribes the abiding practice of continuing to cry out to God even in unrelieved darkness, rather than concluding that unanswered prayer means unheard prayer.

debates.question: Why does this psalm, alone among the laments, end without any turn to praise or resolution?

views.label: It reflects the honest reality of some seasons of suffering that do not resolve neatly
views.case: Scripture includes this psalm precisely because some seasons of affliction, illness, or grief genuinely do not resolve within a single prayer, and God's people need permission to pray honestly through them without forcing premature praise.

views.label: Its placement beside Psalm 89 intentionally deepens the crisis that Book III resolves only in the wider story of God's covenant faithfulness
views.case: Coming just before Psalm 89's lament over the apparently failed Davidic covenant, Psalm 88's darkness may be a deliberate literary choice preparing readers for the deeper resolution found only later in the Psalter and ultimately in Christ.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same pastoral truth: unresolved lament is not unfaithful prayer, and the fuller resolution to both personal and covenantal darkness comes only in God's larger redemptive story, culminating in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Heman's life draws near to sheol, the realm of the dead, verse 3, and he counts himself among those descending to the bor, the Pit, verse 4. From the very edge of abaddon, the place of destruction, he still asks whether God's chesed, steadfast love, can be proclaimed there, verse 11, a question that is itself an act of faith even in despair.

typology.type: Forsaken among the dead, cut off from God's care, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ's cry of dereliction from the cross, 'My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?' Matthew 27:46, Mark 15:34.

typology.type: Friends and companions removed, isolation in suffering, verses 8, 18
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the disciples fleeing at Christ's arrest, leaving Him alone in His darkest hour, Mark 14:50.

study.christ: Jesus entered fully into the anguish this psalm voices. Like Heman, He was forsaken by friends who fled, and He cried out from what felt like abandonment by God Himself on the cross, Matthew 27:46. Because Christ has walked through this very darkness, and emerged from the true Pit through resurrection, believers who pray this psalm's unrelieved lament can know that even their darkest, unresolved nights are known and shared by their Savior.
study.god: God is addressed honestly as the God of salvation even while He feels silent and hidden, showing that He can bear the full weight of human anguish and unanswered questions. The psalm does not explain God's silence, but it also never abandons speaking to Him, honoring both His sovereignty and the legitimacy of unresolved lament as genuine prayer.

crossReferences.note: Christ's cry of abandonment from the cross echoes this psalm's rawest anguish and forsakenness.
Jesus offered up prayers and petitions with loud cries and tears, sharing in this psalm's honest desperation.
The disciples' flight at Christ's arrest parallels Heman's experience of friends removed in his hour of need.

study.oneStory: This darkest of psalms gives dignity to unresolved suffering within the story of God's people, and it is answered not by an explanation but by a Person: Christ, who entered the true darkness of death and forsakenness, so that every honest cry from the depths is met by a Savior who has already walked that road and risen from it.

sermon.title: Heman's Cry of Darkness
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through Psalm 88, honoring its unrelieved darkness as genuine, Spirit-given prayer, and showing how Christ's own suffering and cry of abandonment dignify every believer's unresolved lament.

quiz.q: How does Psalm 88 differ from almost every other lament psalm?

options.text: It ends without any turn to praise or resolution, closing in darkness.
options.note: Psalm 88 is unique in the Psalter for offering no relief or resolution by its final verse.

options.text: It is the only psalm with a happy ending.
options.note: The opposite is true; it is notably unresolved and dark to the very end.

options.text: It never addresses God directly.
options.note: The psalm is addressed directly and repeatedly to the LORD throughout.

options.text: It focuses only on the psalmist's isolation from friends.
options.note: Isolation from friends is a major theme, verses 8, 18, but the specific feature named in this question is the total absence of resolution or praise by the psalm's end.

quiz.q: What does Heman describe happening to his friends in verses 8 and 18?

options.text: God removed them from him, leaving him isolated.
options.note: Both verses describe friends and companions being taken away, leaving Heman alone.

options.text: They gathered around him in comfort.
options.note: The psalm describes the opposite, isolation rather than comforting companionship.

options.text: They betrayed him to his enemies.
options.note: The text describes removal and isolation, not active betrayal.

options.text: They became distant and estranged from him.
options.note: This is very close to correct, though the text more specifically says God removed them from him, making him repulsive to them, verses 8, 18.

quiz.q: What rhetorical questions does Heman ask in verses 10-12?

options.text: Whether God's wonders, love, and faithfulness can be known among the dead.
options.note: These verses press God with honest questions about whether His character can be proclaimed from the grave.

options.text: Whether God exists at all.
options.note: The psalm assumes God's existence throughout; the questions concern His action, not His existence.

options.text: Whether Israel will ever have a king again.
options.note: The questions concern death and God's wonders, not kingship.

options.text: Whether God's wonders and faithfulness reach into the darkness of death.
options.note: This is very close to correct and captures the heart of verses 10-12, though the psalm's precise language names wonders, love, and faithfulness reaching the dead specifically, not death in general.

quiz.q: What is the final word of the psalm, in verse 18?

options.text: Darkness is my closest companion.
options.note: The psalm closes on this stark, unresolved note, without a turn to praise.

options.text: The LORD has answered my prayer with joy.
options.note: No such resolution appears; the psalm ends in darkness.

options.text: I will praise You forever.
options.note: This is the pattern of many other psalms, but not the ending of this one.

options.text: The psalmist describes being separated from his beloved and friend.
options.note: This is true, the first half of verse 18, though the verse's very last and most quoted phrase is 'darkness is my closest companion.'

quiz.q: How does Christ's experience dignify the anguish voiced in this psalm?

options.text: Jesus Himself cried out in forsakenness on the cross and was abandoned by friends, entering the same darkness.
options.note: Christ's cry of dereliction and the disciples' flight parallel Heman's own experience of abandonment.

options.text: Christ never experienced suffering like this.
options.note: The Gospels record Jesus experiencing forsakenness and abandonment strikingly similar to this psalm.

options.text: Christ removed the possibility of ever feeling this way again.
options.note: Believers still experience genuine seasons of darkness; Christ's presence in it, not its removal, is the comfort.

options.text: Christ experienced great sorrow during His earthly ministry.
options.note: This is true and closely related, Hebrews 5:7, but the fullest connection to this psalm is Christ's own cry of forsakenness and His disciples fleeing, paralleling Heman's abandonment precisely.

questions: Psalm 88 gives no resolution, only honest lament. Have you ever felt pressure to force a tidy, hopeful ending onto a season of suffering before you were ready?
Heman describes friends removed from him in his darkest hour. Have you experienced isolation in suffering, and how did you, or could you, continue crying out to God through it?
The psalmist keeps praying even though God seems silent, 'day and night I cry out.' What helps you keep praying when you feel you are receiving no answer?
Christ Himself cried out in forsakenness and was abandoned by His closest friends. How does knowing Jesus has walked through this very darkness change how you view your own unresolved pain?
This psalm shows that Scripture makes room for prayer that ends without resolution. How might this change the way you listen to, or respond to, a friend who is in the middle of unresolved grief?

translationNotes.note: Mahalath Leannoth and Maskil are musical or liturgical terms whose precise meaning is uncertain, but they indicate the psalm was composed with careful, skilled artistry despite its content of raw lament.
Abaddon, meaning destruction, is used elsewhere in the Old Testament alongside Sheol as another name for the realm of the dead.
Darkness is my closest companion could also be rendered darkness is my only friend, underscoring the totality of the psalmist's isolation at the poem's close.

Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Tim Keller's message 'Heman's Cry of Darkness' at podcast.gospelinlife.com.
